fields.title: Your First Program
fields.description: Write your very first lines of Python code! Learn how to make Python talk using the print() function.

fields.title: Variables & Data Types
fields.description: Learn how to store information in variables — the building blocks of every program.

fields.title: Making Decisions
fields.description: Teach your programs to make choices using if, elif, and else statements.

fields.title: Loops
fields.description: Make Python repeat things for you using for loops and while loops.

fields.title: Functions
fields.description: Create reusable blocks of code by writing your own functions.

fields.title: Lists & Tuples
fields.description: Store collections of items and learn to work with ordered data.

fields.title: Dictionaries
fields.description: Store data as key-value pairs — like a real dictionary but for your code.

fields.title: String Magic
fields.description: Master text manipulation — slicing, formatting, and transforming strings.

fields.title: What is Python?
fields.content:
# Welcome to Python!

Python is one of the most popular programming languages in the world — and for good reason. It's designed to be **easy to read** and **fun to write**.

## Why Python?

- **It reads like English.** Python code looks almost like writing instructions for a human.
- **It's used everywhere.** Web apps, data science, AI, games, automation — Python does it all.
- **Huge community.** Millions of developers use Python, so help is always available.

## What does Python code look like?

Here's your first peek at Python:

```python
print("Hello, World!")
```

That's it! One line of code, and Python will display `Hello, World!` on the screen.

Compare that to some other languages that need 5-10 lines just to say hello. Python keeps things simple.

## How does it work?

1. You **write** code (instructions for the computer)
2. Python **reads** your code from top to bottom
3. It **executes** each instruction one at a time
4. You see the **result** on screen

Ready to write your first program? Let's go!

fields.title: The print() Function
fields.content:
# Talking to the World with print()

The `print()` function is how Python displays information on screen. It's the most basic — and one of the most useful — tools in Python.

## How it works

```python
print("Hello, World!")
```

The text inside the quotes is called a **string**. Python displays whatever string you put inside `print()`.

## You can print anything!

```python
print("My name is Alex")
print("I am learning Python")
print("2 + 2 =", 2 + 2)
```

Output:
```
My name is Alex
I am learning Python
2 + 2 = 4
```

Notice that last line — `print()` can do math too!

## Important rules

- Text must be wrapped in quotes: `"like this"` or `'like this'`
- The parentheses `()` are required
- Each `print()` starts a new line

## Try it yourself!

Use the code editor below to experiment. Try printing your name, a math equation, or anything you like!
fields.sandbox_code:
# Try it out! Change the text and click Run
print("Hello, World!")
print("Python is awesome!")

fields.title: Practice: print()
fields.content:
# Time to Practice!

Now it's your turn. Complete the exercises below to master the `print()` function.

Remember:
- Text goes inside quotes: `print("your text")`
- Each `print()` creates a new line
- You can print multiple things: `print("Hello", "World")`

fields.instructions: Complete the code below to print `Hello, World!` to the screen.

# Complete the code to print Hello, World!
___("Hello, World!")

fields.title: Print Your Name
fields.instructions:
Write a program that prints `My name is Python` on one line, then `I love coding!` on the next line.

Expected output:
```
My name is Python
I love coding!
```
fields.starter_code:
# Print two lines:
# Line 1: My name is Python
# Line 2: I love coding!
fields.solution_code:
print("My name is Python")
print("I love coding!")

fields.title: Fix the Bug
fields.instructions: The code below has a bug! It should print `Python is fun!` but something is wrong. Find and fix the error.
fields.starter_code:
# Fix the bug in this code
print("Python is fun!"

fields.title: Math Printer
fields.instructions:
Use `print()` to display the result of `7 * 8`.

Hint: You can do math inside print! For example: `print(2 + 3)` outputs `5`.

Expected output:
```
56
```

fields.title: What Are Variables?
fields.content:
# Variables: Storing Information

Imagine you have labeled boxes where you can store things. That's exactly what **variables** are in Python — named containers for storing data.

## Creating a Variable

```python
name = "Alice"
age = 25
height = 5.6
```

That's it! No special keywords, no declarations. Just pick a name, use `=`, and assign a value.

## The `=` Sign

In Python, `=` doesn't mean "equals" like in math. It means **"store this value"**.

```python
score = 100  # Store 100 in the variable called 'score'
```

Think of it as an arrow pointing left: the value on the right gets stored in the name on the left.

## Using Variables

Once you've stored something, you can use it:

```python
name = "Alice"
print(name)        # Output: Alice
print("Hi,", name) # Output: Hi, Alice
```

## Variable Naming Rules

- Must start with a letter or underscore: `name`, `_count`
- Can contain letters, numbers, underscores: `player_1`, `total_score`
- **Cannot** contain spaces: use `my_name` not `my name`
- **Cannot** start with a number: `1st_place` won't work
- Case-sensitive: `Name` and `name` are different variables

## Pro tip: Use descriptive names!

```python
# Bad (what is x?)
x = 30

# Good (clear and readable)
temperature = 30
```

fields.title: Data Types
fields.content:
# Data Types: What Kind of Data?

Python has several types of data. The three most common ones for beginners:

## Strings (str) — Text
```python
name = "Alice"         # Text wrapped in quotes
greeting = 'Hello!'    # Single quotes work too
```

## Integers (int) — Whole Numbers
```python
age = 25
score = 1000
temperature = -5
```

## Floats (float) — Decimal Numbers
```python
height = 5.9
price = 19.99
pi = 3.14159
```

## Checking the Type

Use `type()` to check what kind of data you have:

```python
print(type("hello"))  # <class 'str'>
print(type(42))       # <class 'int'>
print(type(3.14))     # <class 'float'>
```

## Mixing Types

Be careful mixing types! This works:
```python
print("Age:", 25)  # Python is smart enough to handle this
```

But this causes an error:
```python
"Age: " + 25  # Can't add a string and a number!
```

You'll learn how to fix this with **type conversion** soon!

## Try it!

Experiment with different data types in the editor below.
fields.sandbox_code:
# Try creating variables with different data types
name = "Alice"
age = 25
height = 5.9

print(name, "is", age, "years old")
print("Height:", height)
print(type(name))
print(type(age))
print(type(height))

fields.title: Practice: Variables
fields.content:
# Practice Time!

Put your variable knowledge to the test.

Remember:
- Create variables with `=`: `name = "value"`
- Use variables inside `print()`: `print(name)`
- Python has strings (`"text"`), integers (`42`), and floats (`3.14`)

fields.instructions:

Expected output:


fields.title: Predict the Output
fields.instructions:
What will this code print?

```python
x = 5
y = 3
x = x + y
print(x)
```

fields.title: Introduction Card
fields.instructions:
Create three variables:
- `name` set to `"Sam"`
- `age` set to `20`
- `city` set to `"Austin"`

Then print them in this exact format:
```
Name: Sam
Age: 20
City: Austin
```

Hint: You can use commas in print: `print("Label:", variable)`
fields.starter_code:
# Create the three variables


# Print them in the right format
fields.solution_code:
name = "Sam"
age = 20
city = "Austin"
print("Name:", name)
print("Age:", age)
print("City:", city)

fields.expected_output:
My name is Python
I love coding!

fields.description: Should print the formatted introduction card

fields.content: The blank should be filled with a function that displays text on screen...
The function is called `print`. Replace the `___` with `print`.
You'll need two `print()` statements — one for each line.
Make sure the text inside the quotes matches exactly: `"My name is Python"` and `"I love coding!"`
Look carefully at the parentheses. Is something missing?
The closing parenthesis `)` is missing at the end of the line.
You can put math directly inside `print()`. For example: `print(2 + 3)` outputs `5`.
First create the variable: `message = "Hello, Python!"`. Then on the next line, print it.
Use `*` for multiplication in Python. For example: `print(3 * 4)` outputs `12`.
Use commas in print to combine text and variables: `print("Label:", variable)`
You need three print statements. Each one should start with the label (like `"Name:"`) followed by a comma and the variable.
